Valerie's thumb digs deeper into her cuff, scrubbing violently at the invisible blemish. The ghost of her own childhood eviction flashes in her rigid jaw. 

VALERIE
Humanity does not shield you from the elements, Toby. It doesn't pay the premium. 

Valerie steps into his personal space. Her voice drops to a surgical whisper. 

VALERIE
The second you prioritize a bleeding heart over a signed contract, you are the one sleeping on the pavement. Empathy is a depreciating asset. I highly suggest you liquidate it.

She drops her wrist, smoothing her sleeve into a flawless, icy line. The mask snaps firmly back into place. 

VALERIE
I need the keys to the modernist listing. Now.

Toby swallows hard, his defiant posture crumbling. He looks away, reaching over to the vacant desk to meticulously align a scattered row of paperclips parallel to a keyboard.

TOBY
I'll... I'll pull them from the master lockbox. C-can I get you an iced macchiato for the drive?

VALERIE
Just the keys. 

Valerie turns on her heel and pushes through the heavy glass exit doors, marching toward her financial salvation. 

Toby remains anchored to the desk, his trembling fingers obsessively squaring the edges of his paperwork against the cold glass. 

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. LUXURY BROKERAGE - CENTER AISLE - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie stops dead in her tracks, causing Toby to nearly collide with her. She pivots and physically invades Toby's personal space. Unleashing a suffocating barrage of sanitized corporate buzzwords, she leans in and sharply whispers that morality is a luxury only the ultra-rich can afford. She leaves Toby stunned and silent in the aisle.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Thematic Rejection. Valerie doubles down on her lie that wealth is the only shield. (><) Valerie wants to crush Toby's naive worldview vs. Toby wants to hold onto his morals. (+/-) Challenged (-) to Ruthlessly Victorious (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. LUXURY BROKERAGE - CENTER AISLE - CONTINUOUS

Valerie stalks down the immaculate, glass-walled corridor of the brokerage. Her heels strike the polished concrete like a metronome set to a death march. 

Toby scrambles two steps behind her, clutching a thick, leather-bound portfolio against his chest like a shield.

TOBY
Is it possible we misread the seller's disclosure? I was reviewing the structural schematics for the Pendelton property last night, and the load-bearing distribution is... well, it defies standard civic engineering. 

VALERIE
It is a custom subterranean footprint, Toby. It offers unprecedented spatial optimization. 

TOBY
But the sub-basement foundation shouldn't physically be able to support the cantilevered master suite. If Mr. Pendelton hosts one of his gatherings, the kinetic weight alone-- shouldn't we request a secondary inspection?

Valerie STOPS DEAD. 

Toby collides directly into her back. The leather portfolio slips from his grasp, spilling glossy promotional flyers across the top of a sleek, empty marble credenza.

TOBY
(gasping)
Oh, gosh. My apologies, Valerie. I didn't mean to-- 

Valerie remains perfectly still, her back to him. Her right thumb aggressively, obsessively rubs at the pristine white cuff of her left sleeve. Scrubbing an invisible stain.

VALERIE
Market friction, Toby. That is all an inspection is. Unnecessary friction in a streamlined transaction. 

Toby's hands tremble slightly. He immediately begins gathering the spilled flyers, aligning their edges into flawless, ninety-degree right angles on the credenza. 

TOBY
I-I just think Mr. Pendelton deserves full transparency. If we play by the rules, the system protects the buyer. It protects us. It’s the ethical protocol.

Valerie slowly turns. Her icy gaze drops to the perfectly squared stack of paper, then back up to his earnest, panicked face. 

VALERIE
Look at the veining in this marble, Toby. 

Toby blinks, completely thrown. He looks at the credenza.

TOBY
The... the marble?

VALERIE
Calacatta Gold. Imported directly from Tuscany. Notice how the chaotic, violent pressure of the earth is sanitized into an aesthetic asset? Flawless ROI. It doesn't ask questions about the tectonic plates that crushed it. It simply exists to elevate the space.

TOBY
I-I don't understand what that has to do with the structural integrity of--

Valerie steps forward, physically invading his personal space. Toby shrinks back, his spine pressing against the edge of the marble. 

She leans in. Her voice drops to a sharp, suffocating whisper. 

VALERIE
Ethics are an unmonetized liability. A vanity metric for those who already hold equity. We are facilitating a high-yield lifestyle transition for a premium demographic. 

TOBY
(stuttering)
B-but it's not right. We have a moral obligation to--

VALERIE
Morality is a luxury amenity. It is the imported Italian marble of the human condition. And frankly? Only the ultra-rich possess the liquid capital to afford it. 

Toby stares at her, his mouth slightly open. He compulsively taps the edge of the flyers, making sure they are perfectly flush with the table. 

VALERIE
We are not engineers. We are not saviors. We are the architects of their perceived value. Do not introduce structural doubt into a closed-loop ecosystem. Am I understood?

Toby swallows hard. He avoids her eyes, staring desperately at the floor.

TOBY
I... I could get you a coffee? An espresso? 

VALERIE
Cancel Pendelton’s walkthrough contingencies. Fast-track the escrow. 

Valerie pivots on her heel and resumes her march down the aisle, her posture rigid, her cuffs immaculate. 

Toby remains pinned against the credenza, alone in the center aisle, his hands hovering over the perfectly squared stack of lies. 

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. LUXURY BROKERAGE - HALLWAY - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.narrative_action: An executive assistant flags Valerie down, pointing silently toward the cavernous glass-walled office at the end of the hall. Valerie's victorious smirk falters. She squares her shoulders, adjusting her blazer, and marches down the long, sterile corridor, her pace noticeably slowing as she approaches the heavy mahogany door.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Escalation. Moves Valerie from the predator of the bullpen to the potential prey of the executive suite. (><) Valerie wants to maintain her facade vs. the impending dread of the meeting. (+/-) Victorious (+) to Apprehensive (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. LUXURY BROKERAGE - HALLWAY - MOMENTS LATER

Valerie strides out of the bullpen, practically floating. A razor-sharp, victorious smirk plays on her lips. 

An EXECUTIVE ASSISTANT steps directly into her path. Impeccably dressed, clutching a tablet like a shield.

VALERIE
(brisk)
Cancel my two o'clock walkthrough. We are pivoting to high-yield acquisitions for the rest of the quarter. Keep the momentum liquid.

The Assistant does not type a single word. She does not even blink. 

She simply raises a perfectly manicured finger and points down the long, sterile corridor. 

At the end of the hall sits a cavernous glass-walled office. The executive suite. Through the frosted panes, a broad-shouldered silhouette paces like a caged shark. 

Valerie's smirk evaporates. The apex predator of the bullpen instantly registers as prey. 

She glances over her shoulder. Several junior agents are watching her from their cubicles. The mask must remain flawless.

Valerie squares her shoulders. She snaps the lapels of her designer blazer sharply downward, locking her armor into place. 

VALERIE
Excellent. Prime real estate.

She marches down the corridor. Her stilettos strike the polished marble floor with a militant, echoing rhythm. 

Click. Click. Click. 

But as the heavy mahogany door grows larger, the rhythm decays. The sheer gravity of the executive suite pulls at her. The sharp clicks space out, growing noticeably hesitant. 

Click... Click... 

Valerie stops two feet from the brass door handle. 

Her right hand drifts down to her left wrist. Her thumb presses hard into the pristine white silk of her cuff, obsessively scrubbing at an invisible, phantom stain. 

Her breathing turns shallow. Her financial ruin is a ghost breathing down her neck. If she loses this job, she loses everything. 

A beat. She forces her trembling hand away from the cuff. She lifts her chin, plastering an icy, transactional smile back onto her face. 

She grabs the heavy brass handle.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie enters the cavernous office. Richard Sterling stands with his back to her, facing the floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the city. He calmly pours a glass of expensive scotch, completely ignoring the sound of the door closing to establish immediate, suffocating dominance in the silent room.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Power shift. Introduces the primary antagonist in a position of absolute control. (><) Valerie wants acknowledgment vs. Richard wants to diminish her presence. (+/-) Apprehensive (-) to Subordinate (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - CONTINUOUS

The door clicks shut behind VALERIE. The office is a cavernous mausoleum of glass and steel. Floor-to-ceiling windows frame the sprawling, smog-choked city below.

RICHARD STERLING stands with his back to her, a dark silhouette against the skyline.

Silence. The only sound is the heavy, liquid glug of expensive scotch pouring into a crystal tumbler.

Valerie stands perfectly straight, her designer trench coat practically serving as armor. She waits for him to acknowledge her. 

He doesn't. Richard sets the decanter down. Takes a slow sip. 

VALERIE
The Pendleton showing is confirmed for tomorrow evening.

Nothing. Richard traces a finger along the condensation of his glass. The silence is a weapon, designed to suffocate.

Valerie’s thumb twitches. She subtly rubs the edge of her pristine white cuff, scratching at an invisible blemish.

VALERIE
Arthur Pendleton is highly motivated. The property's brutalist aesthetic perfectly aligns with his portfolio.

Richard finally turns. His face is a mask of serene, predatory calm. He bypasses his massive mahogany desk, walking over to a delicate, antique Victorian dollhouse resting on a pedestal.

RICHARD
Do you know why the invasive strangler fig is so successful, Valerie?

He reaches into the dollhouse, using a pair of silver tweezers to meticulously adjust a microscopic dining chair.

RICHARD
It doesn't bother growing its own foundation. It simply wraps around a larger, established tree. It feeds. It smiles. And eventually, the host rots away.

VALERIE
A fascinating structural anomaly. But Pendleton is looking for turnkey, not a teardown.

Richard chuckles, a dry, hollow sound. He drops the tweezers.

RICHARD
Gemma tells me your credit cards are maxed. Something about a third notice of default on your own condo?

Valerie’s jaw tightens. Her thumb digs harder into her cuff, practically fraying the silk. She forces her posture to relax, pivoting her gaze to the dollhouse.

VALERIE
Gemma has a vivid imagination. The craftsmanship on those miniature baseboards, by the way, is exceptional. Period accurate.

Richard’s calm smile vanishes. He closes the distance between them with terrifying speed, stopping mere inches from her face. The smell of scotch and expensive cologne is overpowering.

RICHARD
(whispering)
Nature abhors a vacuum, Valerie. And right now, you smell like a decaying host.

Valerie holds her breath, her eyes locked on his. She refuses to blink, though her fingers remain locked in a death grip on her sleeve.

RICHARD
This mansion is the crown jewel of the quarter. If you drop the ball with Pendleton, I won't just take the listing. I will strip your license and leave you on the pavement.

Valerie stops rubbing her cuff. She lets her hand drop. Her icy, transactional veneer slides perfectly back into place.

VALERIE
You’ll have his signature by midnight tomorrow. The property is a killer. It practically sells itself.

Richard holds her gaze for a long, suffocating beat. He takes a step back, the tension instantly evaporating from his shoulders as if nothing happened.

RICHARD
Good. Close the door on your way out.

He turns his back to her again, returning to his dollhouse. Valerie lingers for a fraction of a second, entirely dismissed.

She turns and exits into the corridor.

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - DESK AREA - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Still facing the window, Richard begins reciting Valerie's secret, mounting personal debts aloud. As he systematically lists her financial failures, Valerie's composed veneer shatters. Her breathing hitches, she obsessively rubs her expensive blazer cuff to wipe away an invisible stain, and her hands white-knuckle the back of a leather guest chair.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Stripping the armor. Richard attacks Valerie's deepest psychological fear (poverty and exposure). (><) Valerie wants to hide her ruin vs. Richard wants to expose it. (+/-) Subordinate (-) to Panicked (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - DESK AREA - CONTINUOUS

Richard stands with his back to the room, gazing out the floor-to-ceiling glass at the sprawling Los Angeles grid. The smoggy sunlight reflects off his perfectly tailored suit. 

Valerie stands a few feet behind him. She radiates an icy, professional stillness, her posture impeccable.

RICHARD
Nature is remarkably efficient, Valerie. The canopy absorbs the sunlight, and the underbrush... well. It withers.

He doesn't turn around. 

RICHARD
Two hundred and fourteen thousand dollars.

Valerie’s jaw tightens. She breaks her gaze from his back and focuses on the mahogany desk. A partially restored, 19th-century miniature Victorian dollhouse sits under a glass cloche.

VALERIE
The Georgian sash windows on your miniature estate are exquisite. Authentic Baltic birch? The market for heritage miniatures is yielding unprecedented returns right now. A highly liquid asset.

Richard ignores the pivot. 

RICHARD
Sixty-two thousand on the platinum card. Ninety thousand in private, unsecured loans with interest rates that suggest a profound lack of survival instinct. 

Valerie’s breathing hitches. A microscopic intake of air, but in the dead silence of the executive office, it echoes.

RICHARD
Three months delinquent on the leased S-Class. 

VALERIE
I am currently executing a strategic restructuring of my liquid assets. It is a standard portfolio correction.

RICHARD
And the final eviction warning on your luxury loft? Is that a correction?

Valerie stares down at her wrist. Her thumb finds the edge of her pristine silk blazer cuff. She begins to rub it. Hard. Trying to erase an invisible stain.

VALERIE
My landlord and I are simply renegotiating the lease terms to better reflect the property's current market valuation. 

RICHARD
Capital is a living organism, Valerie. It instinctively flees from disease. And right now? You are terminal.

Richard finally turns from the window. He wears a calm, unnerving smile. He steps slowly toward her, his custom oxfords sinking silently into the plush rug.

RICHARD
The ecosystem doesn't tolerate weakness. When a predator senses a tremor in the water, it doesn't ask for a balance sheet. It just bites.

Valerie scrubs at her cuff. The silk begins to fray under her manic, repetitive friction. Her knuckles turn stark white as her other hand grips the back of the heavy leather guest chair.

VALERIE
My financial architecture is entirely sound. The ROI on my current strategy will compound by Q3.

RICHARD
You are a single missed payment away from the street. From being exactly what you've spent your entire adult life pretending not to be.

He stops inches from her. The calm smile never wavers. 

RICHARD
But the universe provides. You stumbled into the Crestview mansion listing. A generational property. A feast. 

Valerie’s grip on the chair squeaks against the leather. 

VALERIE
That property requires highly specialized, bespoke curation. The seller demands my specific aesthetic alignment. 

RICHARD
The seller demands a closer. Not a scavenger. You will transfer the exclusive rights to my desk by tomorrow morning. 

Valerie’s chest rises and falls in rapid, shallow bursts. She stares at the frayed silk of her cuff, rubbing it so hard the skin underneath flushes red.

VALERIE
I am the listing agent of record. The commission structure is locked.

Richard tilts his head, his smile widening just a fraction.

RICHARD
Then I suppose I will terminate your contract with this brokerage immediately. I'll make a few calls to my friends at the banks. Let the ecosystem do what it does best to the underbrush.

Valerie freezes. Her thumb stops moving. The panic finally breaches her eyes, raw and unmasked. 

RICHARD
(softly)
Transfer the listing, Valerie. Or let the tide pull you under.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - WINDOW - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Richard finally turns, his eyes completely dead. He crosses the room, stepping uncomfortably close to Valerie—close enough for her to smell the liquor on his breath. Through clenched teeth, he delivers his ultimate threat: she is a financial liability on the verge of termination. He steps back, leaving her trembling, a ticking clock now attached to her total ruin.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Inciting Incident / Set-up Climax. Establishes the ticking clock that will drive Valerie to the monster house. (><) Richard wants to crush her independence vs. Valerie desperately wants to survive. (+/-) Panicked (-) to Doomed (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - WINDOW - CONTINUOUS

Richard Sterling stands with his back to the room, his broad shoulders silhouetted against the sprawling Los Angeles skyline. 

Instead of admiring the city he owns a fraction of, his massive hands delicately maneuver a pair of silver tweezers inside an antique, meticulously crafted Victorian dollhouse resting on a mahogany pedestal.

Valerie Vance stands ten feet behind him. Her posture is rigid, an expensive tailored blazer acting as armor over her mounting desperation.

VALERIE
The Q3 downturn is nothing more than a temporary market correction, Richard. I’m aggressively repositioning my portfolio to target high-yield legacy buyers. The momentum is shifting.

Richard places a microscopic porcelain teacup onto a tiny dining room table. He doesn’t turn around.

RICHARD
Nature abhors a vacuum, Valerie. But it absolutely despises a weak predator.

VALERIE
I’m not weak. I’m recalibrating. The Palisades listing alone will cover the quarterly spread.

RICHARD
The Palisades listing is a bloated corpse. And the vultures are already circling the carcass.

Valerie’s thumb drops to the cuff of her white silk blouse. She rubs at an invisible stain on the fabric. Hard.

VALERIE
It’s a transitional asset. It just requires the right staging and a visionary buyer.

Richard finally steps away from the dollhouse. He moves to a crystal decanter on his polished desk, pouring two fingers of amber liquid. He doesn't offer her a glass.

RICHARD
I brought you into this firm because you had teeth. You were a shark. Now? You’re a remora, feeding off the scraps of Gemma’s overflow.

Valerie’s eyes dart around the room, desperate to anchor onto anything else. They land back on the miniature Victorian mansion.

VALERIE
The craftsmanship on this piece is extraordinary. The structural integrity of the miniature wainscoting... the aesthetic value alone must yield an astronomical ROI at auction.

Richard stops mid-sip. 

He lowers the crystal glass. The sophisticated, unbothered smirk completely melts off his face.

Richard turns. His eyes are completely dead.

He crosses the expansive Persian rug with alarming, predatory speed. 

Valerie freezes as he steps directly into her personal space. He towers over her, forcing her back until her calves hit the edge of a leather sofa.

Too close. 

The heavy, sweet stench of expensive scotch radiates off his breath. 

Valerie’s hand stops scrubbing her cuff. Her knuckles turn white.

RICHARD
(whispering)
Do not appraise my hobbies, Valerie.

Valerie holds her breath, her icy professional mask cracking at the edges.

RICHARD
(clenched teeth)
You think I don't see the water rising around your neck? The missed car payments. The credit inquiries. You are bleeding out on my pristine floors.

VALERIE
My financials are entirely--

RICHARD
You are a financial liability. 

He leans in a fraction of an inch closer. 

RICHARD
You have until the end of the month to close a tier-one property. If you don't, I will terminate your contract, strip your remaining escrows, and personally ensure you never sell so much as a two-bedroom condo in this city again.

Valerie stares up at him. For a split second, the polished cutthroat vanishes, leaving only a terrified, powerless child staring down a landlord.

RICHARD
Do we have an understanding?

Valerie forces a swallow. She gives a single, stiff nod.

Richard holds her gaze for one agonizing second longer. 

Then, he steps back. The oppressive weight lifts.

He turns his back on her, walking calmly to the window to admire his tiny, perfect, controllable world.

RICHARD
Close the door on your way out. The draft is upsetting the curtains.

Valerie remains rooted to the spot. Her hands tremble violently against her sides. 

She looks down at her pristine, perfectly clean cuffs. Then at the heavy oak door. 

The clock is ticking.

scenes.scene_heading: INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - WINDOW - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie stands trembling as Richard steps back, leaving her with the ticking clock of total ruin. Her flawless professional veneer shatters for a split second. She obsessively scrubs at an invisible stain on her expensive cuff, her breathing shallow. Desperate, she pulls out her phone, aggressively swiping through dead client leads until a cryptic, anonymous message pings. It contains nothing but an address for an unlisted property in a high-net-worth zip code. Recognizing this as her absolute last lifeline, she straightens her blazer, burying her panic, and storms out of the office.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Establishes the immediate aftermath of the previous sequence and propels Valerie toward the catalyst location. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to regain her physical composure versus her overwhelming panic of imminent financial ruin. Value Shift: From trapped and terrified (-) to desperately proactive (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. RICHARD'S OFFICE - WINDOW - CONTINUOUS

Richard stands impossibly close to Valerie. The air between them is suffocating. He invades her personal space, his pristine tailored chest practically brushing her silk blouse.

Valerie stares straight ahead. Her jaw is locked. 

RICHARD
(whispering)
The apex predator doesn’t negotiate with the gazelle, Valerie. It just eats. 

Richard slowly leans back, restoring the physical boundary. His sophisticated, unbothered smile returns. 

RICHARD
Forty-eight hours. If your pipeline doesn’t show a pulse by Friday, I’m reassigning your desk to Gemma. The ecosystem always corrects itself.

He turns and glides toward the heavy mahogany double doors. 

RICHARD
Close the door on your way out. The draft is terrible for the orchids.

The heavy doors CLICK shut. 

Valerie is alone. 

Below the floor-to-ceiling glass, the Los Angeles traffic crawls like a river of blood-red taillights. 

Valerie’s flawless posture holds for three seconds. 

Then, she shatters. 

A violent tremble seizes her hands. Her breathing turns shallow, ragged. She looks down at her right wrist. 

Her left thumb finds the edge of her expensive, stark-white French cuff. She begins to scrub it. Hard. There is no stain, but her thumb digs into the fabric, rubbing frantically, trying to erase an invisible blemish. 

The friction turns her own skin red. 

She forces her eyes away from the cuff and looks out the window. 

A large, green JUNE BUG lies on its back on the marble windowsill, its legs kicking desperately against the glass, unable to right itself. 

Valerie’s frantic breathing hitches. She stares at the dying insect. 

She reaches out with a trembling hand, picks up a piece of Richard’s gold-embossed heavy-stock stationery, and gently slides it under the bug. 

With surgical care, she lifts the struggling insect, opens the window latch a fraction of an inch, and tilts the paper. The bug tumbles out into the smoggy wind. 

She slams the latch shut. 

Valerie whips out her phone. Her thumb swipes rapidly. 

ON THE SCREEN: A banking app. OVERDRAFT WARNING. BALANCE: -$432.10. 

She immediately swipes away the notification. She pulls up her contacts. Selects a number. Hits call. 

She presses the phone to her ear. Her left thumb goes right back to scrubbing her white cuff. 

MALE VOICE (O.S.)
(through phone)
Hello? 

VALERIE
(smooth, icy perfection)
Marcus. Valerie Vance. I was just reviewing the portfolio analytics for the Brentwood parcel, and I noticed the mid-century sightlines are severely undervalued in the current appraisal. We need to pivot our offer. 

MARCUS (O.S.)
(through phone)
Valerie. Look. Gemma Frost just showed us a turnkey in Bel Air. We’re going with her. 

Valerie’s thumb digs so hard into her cuff the button snaps off and clatters onto the marble floor. 

VALERIE
Marcus, Gemma doesn’t understand the foundational equity of a modernist--

MARCUS (O.S.)
(through phone)
We’re done, Valerie. Lose my number. 

A dial tone echoes from the speaker. 

Valerie lowers the phone. Her reflection in the glass is pale, hollow. The ghost of her childhood poverty stares back at her. 

She raises her hand, ready to hurl the phone against the bulletproof glass. 

PING. 

She freezes. A text message notification glows on the screen. 

UNKNOWN SENDER. 

She opens it. There is no text. No greeting. Just a single line. 

"9944 CROWLEY DRIVE. EXCLUSIVE LISTING. VACANT. DOOR IS UNLOCKED."

Valerie frowns. She highlights the address and pastes it into her MLS app. 

A loading wheel spins. 

The property data populates. 

ON THE SCREEN: ZESTIMATE - $22,500,000. 

Valerie’s eyes widen. The trembling in her hands instantly stops. 

She stares at the astronomical number. A twenty-two million dollar listing. A three percent commission. 

Six hundred and seventy-five thousand dollars. 

The ragged breathing vanishes. Her spine straightens, vertebra by vertebra. The terrified, trapped animal disappears, swallowed whole by the cutthroat shark. 

Valerie looks down at her missing cuff button. She rolls the sleeve up, hiding the imperfection. She rolls the other sleeve up to match. 

Symmetrical. Perfect. 

She turns on her heel and strides toward the mahogany doors. 

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. VALERIE'S LUXURY SEDAN - DAY
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie speeds her luxury sedan down a winding, heavily wooded road, her knuckles white on the leather steering wheel. She sanitizes her fear by loudly reciting sterile real estate buzzwords to herself in the rearview mirror, psyching herself up for whatever this cryptic listing entails. The GPS on her dashboard loses signal, the screen glitching as the ancient trees grow unnaturally thick and dark around the speeding car.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Builds kinetic tension and transitions Valerie from her sterile corporate world into the isolated, supernatural realm of the monster. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to maintain psychological control versus the increasingly isolating, hostile environment. Value Shift: From determined (+) to unsettled (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. VALERIE'S LUXURY SEDAN - DAY

A pristine, aggressively leased BMW tears down a winding ribbon of asphalt, cutting through a tunnel of ancient, rotting oaks. 

Inside, the cabin is a sterile fortress of cream leather and brushed steel. VALERIE VANCE grips the steering wheel. Her knuckles are bone-white. 

She locks eyes with her own reflection in the rearview mirror. Her icy, flawless facade is rigid, but her breathing is shallow. Fast. 

VALERIE
(to the mirror)
It’s not isolated. It’s an exclusive enclave. Unprecedented privacy. A bespoke sanctuary for the high-net-worth buyer.

She takes a sharp turn. The tires SQUEAL, fighting for traction. 

Outside, the forest begins to mutate. The trees grow unnaturally thick, their gnarled branches interlocking above the road to choke out the midday sun. 

Heavy, bruised shadows slice rhythmically across Valerie’s face. 

VALERIE
Turnkey condition. Motivated seller. The market is correcting, but luxury... luxury is immune to the downturn. You are immune to the downturn.

On the dashboard, the GPS screen stutters. The glowing blue route line fragments into a jagged, pixelated mess. 

The automated voice stammers through the luxury speakers. 

GPS VOICE (V.O.)
Recalculating... rec-rec-recalculating...

VALERIE
Do not do this. Not today. 

She jabs a manicured finger at the glossy screen. 

The GPS emits a high-pitched, digital SQUEAL. A web of static consumes the map, and the screen dies. Pitch black. 

The sudden silence in the cabin is suffocating, broken only by the crunch of dead leaves beneath the tires. 

Valerie stares at the dead screen. The road ahead narrows into a claustrophobic canyon of twisted bark and creeping fog. 

The panic rises. A microscopic crack in the ice. 

Her right hand drops from the steering wheel. Her thumb and forefinger find the edge of her crisp, white silk cuff. 

She begins to rub it. Obsessively. Scrubbing at a phantom stain, her nails digging into her own wrist. 

VALERIE
(whispering)
Maximized ROI. Immediate liquidity. Generational wealth. 

She scrubs harder. The silk begins to fray under the manic friction. 

She looks up from the cuff to the imposing, darkened road ahead. If she turns back, there is nothing but the abyss of her bank account. 

Valerie’s jaw clenches. She shifts the console into sport mode. 

She slams her designer heel onto the accelerator. The engine ROARS, plunging the sedan deeper into the belly of the woods.

scenes.scene_heading: EXT. BRUTALIST MANSION - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: The luxury sedan crunches to a halt on a pristine gravel driveway. Valerie steps out, dwarfed by a stunning, brutalist modernist mansion sitting silently among the dense trees. She approaches the towering concrete and glass structure, her hopeless desperation driving her forward step by step. Finding a heavy lockbox on the massive front doors, she punches in a default code and extracts a sleek, unnaturally cold black key.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Introduces the monster in its dormant state, showcasing the physical scale of the property. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to evaluate the property's financial worth versus the imposing, unnatural silence of the brutalist architecture. Value Shift: From unsettled (-) to awe-struck (+).
scenes.draft_text:
EXT. BRUTALIST MANSION - CONTINUOUS

Tires grind against crushed white marble. A leased black Audi sedan jerks to a halt in the center of a pristine, circular driveway.

The engine cuts out.

Silence. Absolute, dead silence. No birds. No wind in the dense, suffocating pines that wall off the property from the rest of the world.

VALERIE VANCE (30s) steps out. Her tailored designer suit is an armor of sharp lines and neutral tones.

She slowly looks up.

THE MANSION looms. A staggering, monolithic fortress of raw, board-formed concrete and black, reflective glass. It doesn’t sit on the landscape; it dominates it. Brutalist, jagged overhangs jut out like the ribs of a prehistoric leviathan.

Valerie is entirely dwarfed by the structure. She stands frozen.

Her phone vibrates violently in her hand. 

The screen flashes: "FINAL NOTICE - ASSET SEIZURE".

Her pristine facade cracks. She aggressively scrubs her thumb over the cuff of her silk blouse, attacking a microscopic, imaginary blemish. She scrubs until the skin of her wrist turns red.

She hits ignore. Shoves the phone deep into her pocket. The mask slides back into place. Cold. Transactional.

VALERIE
(muttering)
Highest price per square foot in the county. Focus.

She marches toward the entrance. Her stiletto heels click rhythmically against the concrete pavers. Each step echoes off the massive, windowless walls, sounding like a gavel striking wood.

She stops.

At the edge of the path, a large, iridescent beetle lies trapped on its back, legs thrashing desperately against the smooth concrete.

Valerie stares at it. The ruthless capitalist pauses.

Careful not to scuff her Louboutins, she kneels. She slides the edge of a heavy-stock platinum business card under the beetle, gently flipping it over. It scurries into the damp moss.

Valerie stands, brushing a speck of dust from her skirt.

She continues her approach. The temperature drops noticeably as she steps under the shadow of the massive, cantilevered concrete awning.

She taps the side of her smartwatch, activating a voice memo.

VALERIE
(into watch)
Initial exterior assessment. The architecture is... imposing. Aggressive brutalism. Zero curb appeal for a traditional family.

She pauses, looking at the sheer, impenetrable walls.

VALERIE
(into watch)
But the privacy features are unparalleled. Floor-to-ceiling tinted glazing. It doesn't just offer seclusion. It offers absolute immunity. We market this to the apex predators. Tech, hedge funds. Someone who wants to buy a fortress.

She reaches the front doors. Ten feet tall, forged from solid, unvarnished ebony wood.

Hanging from the oxidized iron handle is a heavy, industrial lockbox.

Valerie grips it. She flinches.

The metal is freezing. Condensation beads against her palm.

She punches in the standard realtor code: 1-2-3-4.

The buttons stick. She presses harder, her perfectly manicured nails digging into the keypad.

VALERIE
Come on. Don't do this. Not today.

She punches it again. 0-0-0-0.

A deep, low, sub-bass THUD reverberates through the soles of her shoes. It doesn't come from the box. It comes from deep inside the belly of the house.

The lockbox springs open with a sharp hiss.

Inside rests a single key. It is matte black, devoid of any metallic shine, resembling carved obsidian.

Valerie reaches in. She pinches the bow of the key.

She gasps, yanking her hand back.

It’s unnaturally cold. Like dry ice burning the skin.

She hesitates, her financial desperation warring with a primal instinct to turn around and run back to the car.

Her phone buzzes in her pocket again. A relentless, vibrating reminder of the abyss waiting for her.

Valerie sets her jaw. She reaches back into the box and grips the key tightly, enduring the biting cold.

She pulls it into the light. The black metal seems to swallow the sun, casting no reflection whatsoever.

The unsettling silence of the woods fades away. The crushing weight of her debt evaporates. As she stares at the key, her breathing slows.

Her eyes trace the towering, jagged concrete silhouette against the grey sky. 

A slow, awestruck smile breaks through her icy veneer.

VALERIE
(whispering)
You are magnificent.

She slides the black key into the deadbolt.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION FOYER - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie unlocks and pushes open the heavy entryway. The moment she steps inside, the ambient temperature plummets unnaturally, her breath suddenly visible in the air. She looks down the long, pristine hallway. The sharp architectural lines of the walls seem to subtly shift and warp inward, creating a dizzying optical illusion of a closing throat. She physically stumbles back, clutching her chest as vertigo hits her.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: The supernatural catalyst physically manifests, directly assaulting the protagonist's senses. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to assess the interior layout versus the house actively disorienting her physical perception. Value Shift: From awe-struck (+) to deeply alarmed (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION FOYER - CONTINUOUS

Valerie leans her entire body weight against the oversized, custom-milled oak door. The heavy deadbolts disengage with a loud, industrial thud.

She pushes. The door drags across the threshold, groaning like a waking beast. 

Valerie steps inside. The door swings shut behind her. The latch engages with the heavy, airtight finality of a bank vault.

Silence. Immediate and absolute.

Valerie takes a breath. A thick plume of white vapor escapes her lips, hanging in the air.

She stops. Her brow furrows. She exhales again. More vapor.

VALERIE
(taps her earpiece)
Toby, voicemail again. Are you screening my calls? If you are, stop it. I’m inside the property.

She pulls a sleek, silver laser measuring tape from the pocket of her tailored blazer. She aims it down the long, pristine hallway. 

VALERIE
Tell the seller’s rep they need to recalibrate the HVAC immediately. It’s a literal freezer in here. We are trying to sell bespoke warmth, not a meat locker.

The red dot of the laser dances across the imported Brazilian slate. 

VALERIE
Sightlines are... aggressive. Uninterrupted flow from the foyer to the atrium. It’s almost too sterile. We’ll market it as uncompromising modernist flow. 

She walks forward. Her Louboutins click sharply against the stone. A sharp, rhythmic echo.

VALERIE
The acoustics are a nightmare, but the buyers won't notice until after escrow clears. Just get the staging team to bring in some organic textures. Ferns. Maybe some distressed velvet to absorb the sound.

Valerie pauses. She looks down at the baseboard.

A massive, bruised stag beetle struggles on its back, its legs kicking helplessly against the freezing slate. 

Valerie’s icy, transactional veneer drops. 

She glances over her shoulder, ensuring she is completely alone. 

Crouching down, she winces as her expensive pencil skirt pulls dangerously tight against her thighs. She pulls a thick, metallic business card from her blazer.

With surprising, meticulous tenderness, she slides the card under the struggling insect. 

She lifts the beetle, carrying it over to a massive potted monstera plant in the corner of the foyer. She gently deposits it into the dark soil.

VALERIE
(softly)
There you go. Stay out of sight. The market is brutal today.

She stands, brushing invisible dirt from her hands.

The ambient temperature plummets again. Drastically.

Frost webs across the edges of the slate tiles, crawling rapidly toward the tips of her designer heels.

Valerie shivers, wrapping her arms around herself. She looks down the long hallway.

The sharp, geometric lines of the walls begin to blur. 

The crown molding bows inward. The baseboards curve up. 

The walls subtly warp. Contracting. Expanding.

In. Out. In. Out.

An impossible optical illusion. The hallway is transforming into a massive, concrete throat. And it is closing in on her.

Valerie’s breath catches. The floor tilts beneath her feet.

Vertigo hits her like a physical blow. The world spins.

She stumbles backward, her arms flailing wildly. Her heel catches the thick edge of the entryway runner. 

She slams hard against the heavy oak door, clutching her chest. Her heart hammers violently against her ribs. She gasps for air, her eyes wide with primal terror.

She stares down the corridor.

The walls are perfectly straight. Pristine. Unmoving.

The frost is gone.

Valerie stares at her trembling hands. The facade is cracked.

She immediately grabs the left cuff of her immaculate silk blouse. She scrubs at it. Hard. Digging her manicured nails into the fabric to remove a stain that isn't there.

Her breathing is ragged. She scrubs faster, the expensive silk beginning to fray under her desperate friction.

VALERIE
(whispering, breathless)
It’s just... recessed lighting. The LEDs.

She keeps scrubbing. Her knuckles turn white.

VALERIE
It creates a dynamic spatial illusion. A trick of the eye. Completely normal in brutalist spaces.

She forces her hands to her sides. She clenches them into fists so tight her nails dig into her palms. 

VALERIE
(louder, voice shaking)
High-yield architectural dynamism. It's a feature. Not a flaw.

Valerie swallows hard. She pushes herself off the door, refusing to look back at the lock. 

She stares into the dark, silent belly of the house. 

Deep in the shadows of the hallway, the red dot of her dropped laser measure rests on the floor. Waiting.

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION LIVING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: A dark, predatory presence vibrates deep within the pristine hardwood floorboards, sending a literal tremor up through Valerie's high heels. Her survival instincts scream at her; she backs toward the open front door, ready to bolt. She stops on the threshold. Pulling out her phone, she opens her calculator app, rapidly crunching the numbers on a six-percent commission for a property of this scale. Staring at the life-saving number on the screen, she forcefully pushes her dread aside, pockets the phone, and steps fully into the belly of the beast, pulling the heavy front door shut behind her.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Cements Valerie's fatal flaw by showing her actively choosing greed over her own physical safety. Micro-Conflict: Valerie's biological survival instinct versus her desperate need for financial security. Value Shift: From terrified (-) to ruthlessly resolved (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION LIVING ROOM - CONTINUOUS

The modernist architecture is flawless. Floor-to-ceiling glass, exposed steel beams, sprawling Brazilian walnut floors. 

But the air is too thick. The light bends wrong in the corners.

A SUBSONIC THRUM vibrates deep within the pristine hardwood. 

It travels up the stiletto heel of VALERIE VANCE (30s). 

Valerie freezes. Her knuckles turn bone-white around her leather portfolio. 

The vibration intensifies. It’s not a draft. It’s not settling pipes. It feels like a heartbeat. A massive, hungry one.

VALERIE
(whispering to herself)
Incredible radiant heating. Top of the line.

She isn't convincing herself. Her breathing quickens. 

Valerie aggressively scrubs at the cuff of her silk blouse, trying to erase a stain that isn't there. 

A sudden, wet CRACK echoes from the hallway. Like a snapping tendon.

Valerie flinches. Her survival instinct overrides her posture. She takes a slow, involuntary step backward. 

Another step. The house seems to stretch around her. The shadows pool like dark water. 

She retreats toward the foyer. Her designer heels click in rapid, frantic succession across the hardwood. 

She hits the threshold of the open front door. 

Outside, the world is indifferent. Birds chirp. The California sun is blindingly bright. Safety. 

Valerie stands on the precipice. One foot on the welcome mat, one foot on the vibrating floor. 

She reaches backward for the heavy brass handle. Ready to bolt. 

Then, she looks down. 

A struggling WASP spins in circles on the floorboards near her shoe. Injured wing. 

Valerie’s icy veneer cracks. A flash of genuine, misplaced empathy. She drops to one knee, pulling a thick, embossed business card from her blazer. 

She gently slides the card toward the insect.

VALERIE
(softly)
Come on. Up you go. Let's relocate you. 

The floorboards beneath the wasp suddenly SEPARATE. A millimeter gap. 

A tiny, wet SQUELCH. 

The wasp is sucked down into the dark seam. 

The floorboards snap perfectly shut. Not a trace. 

Valerie violently recoils. She scrambles backward on her hands and knees until her spine hits the doorframe. 

Her chest heaves. Her eyes are wide with primal, unadulterated terror. The house is alive. It eats. 

She grabs the doorframe, pulling herself up. She has to leave. Now. 

Her phone BUZZES in her pocket. 

She yanks it out, fingers trembling wildly. 

ON THE SCREEN: A push notification. 

OVERDRAFT ALERT: ACCOUNT ENDING IN 4092. BALANCE: -$84.12.

A second notification pops up immediately after: 

URGENT: FINAL EVICTION WARNING - PAYMENT REQUIRED.

The words "EVICTION WARNING" reflect in Valerie's wide, terrified eyes. 

The memory of her childhood trauma washes over her face. The trembling in her hands shifts. It’s no longer fear of the house. It’s revulsion at her own poverty. 

Valerie aggressively swipes away the banking app. 

She opens the CALCULATOR APP. 

Her thumbs fly across the glass screen, fueled by sheer, desperate adrenaline. 

45,000,000.

Multiply. 

0.06.

She hits equals. 

$2,700,000.

Two point seven million dollars. 

The subsonic thrum of the house continues. The floorboards vibrate against her soles. The shadows creep closer to the foyer. 

Valerie stares at the screen. The life-saving number glows in the dimming light. 

Slowly, her trembling stops. Her breathing evens out. 

She locks the phone screen. Slips it back into her tailored pocket. 

Valerie looks back into the cavernous, hungry living room. Her terrified expression melts away, replaced by an unbreakable, icy professional veneer. 

She brushes a speck of invisible dust off her lapel. 

VALERIE
It's a seller's market.

Valerie steps off the welcome mat. She walks fully into the belly of the beast. 

She grabs the heavy brass handle of the front door. 

With a decisive, echoing THUD, Valerie pulls the door shut behind her. 

The deadbolt CLICKS.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: EXT. BRUTALIST MANSION - THE NEXT AFTERNOON
scenes.narrative_action: A loud, obnoxious sports car aggressively revs up the gravel driveway, spitting rocks into the grass. Arthur Pendelton steps out, immediately booming into his phone about an obscure luxury purchase, completely ignoring his surroundings. Valerie waits by the grand doors, her icy professional veneer perfectly in place, meticulously masking her underlying anxiety about re-entering the house.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Introduces the catalyst victim and establishes the high-stakes reality of the showing. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to initiate a controlled, professional tour versus Arthur's chaotic, space-consuming energy. Value Shift: From anxious anticipation (-) to transactional control (+).
scenes.draft_text:
EXT. BRUTALIST MANSION - THE NEXT AFTERNOON

A monolithic slab of poured concrete and black glass. It doesn't sit on the landscape; it crushes it.

VALERIE VANCE (30s) stands at the base of the sweeping front steps. Impeccably tailored cream suit. Flawless posture. 

Her right thumb aggressively, rhythmically rubs at an invisible stain on her left French cuff. Rubbing. Scraping.

A roar of a V12 engine shatters the silence. 

A neon-green McLaren SENNA tears up the pristine gravel driveway. It doesn't slow down until the last possible second, slamming its brakes and spitting jagged rocks across the manicured lawn.

Valerie stops rubbing her cuff. Her hands clasp in front of her. The icy veneer locks into place.

The McLaren's dihedral door swings up. 

ARTHUR PENDELTON (40s) steps out. He wears a custom Brioni jacket over a vintage band tee, AirPods jammed in his ears. 

ARTHUR
(shouting into the air)
I don't care if the Geneva broker is asleep, wake him up! If it’s not the Patek Philippe Grandmaster Chime, I don't want it! I am not paying seven figures for a standard complication like some crypto peasant!

He slams the car door. He doesn't look at Valerie. He doesn't look at the house. He paces the gravel, kicking a stone.

VALERIE
Mr. Pendelton. Welcome to--

ARTHUR
(into phone)
Tell him I'll buy the whole boutique and fire him if he doesn't source it by Tuesday. Yeah. Ciao. 

Arthur rips the AirPods out. He finally looks up at the towering concrete structure. He lets out a booming, entirely too loud laugh that echoes off the brutalist walls.

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
Christ! It looks like a supervillain bunker. 

VALERIE
It’s an uncompromising statement piece. Post-war brutalism updated for the modern oligarch. 

Arthur marches past her, completely ignoring the grand steps. He walks right up to the foundation, running a sweaty hand along the raw, textured concrete.

ARTHUR
My ex-wife just bought a twelve-mil Spanish Colonial in Calabasas. Archways. Terracotta. Pathetic. I told her, 'Susan, you have the imagination of a Pinterest board.' 

He turns to Valerie, invading her personal space.

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
I need something that screams. Something that says, 'I own the skyline.' Does this scream, Val?

Valerie doesn't flinch. She gracefully steps sideways, gesturing to the massive, fifteen-foot obsidian front doors. 

VALERIE
The architectural volume speaks for itself, Arthur. The poured-in-place concrete provides an absolute thermal envelope. It’s entirely self-sustaining. You aren't just buying a property. You're acquiring a fortress.

Arthur checks his wrist-- a heavy, diamond-encrusted Audemars Piguet. He taps the glass twice, not actually reading the hands. A subtle crack in his booming bravado. 

ARTHUR
Yeah, well. It better be big inside. I have a lot of... I host a lot of people. Important people. 

VALERIE
The open-concept layout maximizes spatial dominance. You will never feel overlooked here.

Arthur's chest puffs out slightly. The buzzwords hit their mark. The anxiety of his imposter syndrome soothed by the promise of square footage.

ARTHUR
Spatial dominance. I like that. I like that a lot. 

He marches up the steps, taking them two at a time. He reaches the obsidian doors, grabbing the heavy iron handle. He pulls. 

Locked. 

He pulls harder, his face flushing red. 

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
Stuck. 

Valerie glides up the steps behind him. Her thumb twitches toward her cuff, but she forces her hand flat against her thigh. 

VALERIE
It requires a biometric bypass. The security system is highly proprietary. 

She steps between Arthur and the door. She doesn't look at the handle. She stares directly into Arthur's eyes, her smile razor-sharp.

VALERIE (CONT'D)
Before we enter, I must emphasize... the property is highly sensitive to its occupants. It’s an exclusive environment. We move at my pace. 

Arthur laughs, a booming, nervous sound. He checks his watch again. 

ARTHUR
I'm a busy man, Val. Let's see the guts. 

Valerie turns to the door. She places her palm flat against the black glass scanner. 

A deep, low hum vibrates through the concrete beneath their feet. Not a mechanical click, but a deep, resonant thrum. Like a stomach growling.

The heavy obsidian doors slowly glide inward, revealing the pitch-black maw of the foyer. 

Valerie steps aside, gesturing into the darkness with perfect, transactional grace. 

VALERIE
After you.

Arthur steps over the threshold, immediately swallowed by the shadows. 

Valerie takes one last breath of the outside air. Her thumb aggressively scrapes her cuff once, twice. 

She steps inside.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION FOYER / HALLWAYS - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie leads Arthur inside. Arthur booms through the echoing halls, constantly interrupting Valerie's carefully rehearsed real estate buzzwords to brag about his wealth. He treats the magnificent space as a hollow status symbol, wildly gesturing and ignoring Valerie's attempts to guide his physical path. The temperature drops again, frosting the windows, but Arthur is too self-absorbed and loud to notice the environmental shift.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Escalates the tension by placing an obnoxious prey inside the predator's belly, highlighting the stark contrast between human arrogance and supernatural threat. Micro-Conflict: Valerie wants to sell the architectural features versus Arthur's desperate need to dominate the conversation. Value Shift: From in control (+) to frustrated (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION FOYER / HALLWAYS - CONTINUOUS

Cavernous. Brutalist concrete meets impossibly high glass. 

VALERIE VANCE stands in the center of the massive space, perfectly postured, projecting total ownership. 

ARTHUR PENDELTON blows right past her. He doesn't walk; he occupies territory. He aggressively swivels his head, taking in the architecture with the eye of a conqueror rather than a buyer.

VALERIE
Notice the cantilevered entry. The architect designed the spatial compression to naturally draw the--

ARTHUR
It’s a little tight. Honestly? My penthouse in Tribeca has a wider throat. 

Valerie’s smile is razor-thin. She discreetly rubs the edge of her pristine silk cuff. 

VALERIE
It’s a deliberate intimacy. It forces the eye toward the primary gallery. 

She steps forward, extending a graceful hand to guide him down the main artery of the house. 

Arthur ignores the gesture. He wanders off the path, zeroing in on a smooth, load-bearing pillar. He slaps the polished concrete like the hood of a cheap rental car. 

ARTHUR
I’m bringing in my own interior guy anyway. Fendi Casa. Everything in here is too... quiet. I need noise. I need people to walk in and feel completely inadequate. 

He laughs. A booming, aggressive bark that bounces violently off the glass. 

A sudden, sharp DRAFT sweeps through the foyer. 

Valerie’s breath catches. A faint wisp of condensation escapes her lips. She glances at the massive floor-to-ceiling windows. 

Crystalline FROST creeps across the edges of the glass, crawling inward like a spiderweb. 

Arthur doesn’t notice the drop in temperature. He is already marching down the long, shadowed hallway, completely off the curated tour route. 

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
You know what this place needs? A moat. I’m serious. I told my guy at Vacheron Constantin last week, I said, 'If you don't have a moat, you're just camping.' 

Valerie hurries to catch up, her heels clicking sharply against the seamless terrazzo floor. 

VALERIE
The property lines extend all the way to the ridge. You have absolute, unrestricted sovereignty over the topography. 

Arthur stops abruptly. Valerie nearly collides with his broad back. 

He stares into a sunken, darkened lounge area off the main hall. The air here is noticeably heavier. Colder. 

ARTHUR
Is the HVAC broken? I’m not paying eight figures for a broken thermostat. 

VALERIE
It’s a smart-climate ecosystem. It passively adjusts to the biometric heat of its occupants. 

She rubs her cuff again. Harder this time. The fabric pulls dangerously tight against her wrist. 

ARTHUR
Well, tell it I run hot. 

He steps down into the sunken lounge. The shadows seem to cling to his expensive suit, swallowing the ambient light. 

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
(bellowing)
HELLO! 

He waits for the echo. 

Nothing. The house swallows the sound completely. It is unnervingly, impossibly dead. 

Arthur frowns. His booming confidence wavers for a fraction of a second. He aggressively checks his heavy gold watch, tapping the crystal face without actually reading the hands. 

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
Acoustics are garbage. How am I supposed to host the Silicon Valley board if they can't hear me toast from across the room? 

Valerie steps to the edge of the sunken room. She doesn’t go down. Her instincts, buried deep beneath her polished veneer, scream at her to stay on the higher ground. 

She looks at the frost, now fully framing the window behind him. The house is freezing them out. Or preparing to keep its meal fresh. 

She swallows her unease. The commission is the only thing that matters. 

VALERIE
It’s acoustic dampening. Military-grade insulation. It ensures that whatever happens in this house, stays entirely within these walls. 

Arthur looks up at her. The phrase lands. His ego inflates, his chest puffing out as the imposter syndrome recedes. 

VALERIE (CONT'D)
Ultimate discretion. 

ARTHUR
Ultimate discretion. I like that. I can get loud. 

He smirks, turning his back to Valerie to survey the dark corners of the room. He gestures wildly at a pristine, monolithic slate wall. 

ARTHUR (CONT'D)
Yeah. We’re going to tear down that wall. Put in a shark tank. Make sure the paperwork is ready, Val. I don't wait. 

Valerie stares at the back of his head. The frost on the glass behind him thickens into solid, jagged ice. 

VALERIE
(icy)
I’ll draft the addendum. 

Arthur laughs his booming laugh again. The glass in the hallway shudders. 

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION GOURMET KITCHEN - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Arthur aggressively insults the home's craftsmanship, slapping his heavy hand against a pristine marble countertop. His booming voice cracks slightly on a high note, revealing the deep imposter syndrome beneath his bluster. He obsessively checks his heavy luxury watch without actually reading the time, stepping uncomfortably close to Valerie to project dominance. In direct, immediate response to his disrespect, the heavy oak doors leading back to the hallway mysteriously slam shut behind them with bone-rattling force, sealing them in.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: The house reacts to Arthur's presence, shifting from a passive environment to an active, physical threat. Micro-Conflict: Arthur wants to belittle the property to inflate his ego versus the house violently asserting its physical dominance. Value Shift: From arrogant annoyance (-) to sudden, trapped shock (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION GOURMET KITCHEN - CONTINUOUS

Arthur PENDELTON (40s, dressed like a tech-bro yacht captain) paces the length of the kitchen. He drags his heavy hand along the pristine, white marble island. 

He stops. SLAPS his thick palm against the stone.

ARTHUR
You call this bespoke? It feels like compressed chalk.

VALERIE
(icy professional smile)
The veining in that imported Carrara is actually a highly sought-after natural anomaly. It offers an unparalleled aesthetic yield.

ARTHUR
My interior guy, Sven, imports raw basalt straight from Reykjavik. This? This is pedestrian.

Arthur yanks open a custom matte-black cabinet. He slams it shut. The wood rattles.

ARTHUR
Soft-close hinges? What is this, a suburban flip? If I'm dropping eight figures, I want hardware forged by a guy who doesn't even speak English.

Valerie’s smile tightens. Her thumb aggressively rubs at an invisible smudge on her silk cuff. 

VALERIE
The minimalist integration allows the buyer to impose their own architectural narrative. It is essentially a blue-chip canvas for a visionary.

ARTHUR
(loud, echoing laugh)
A canvas? I don’t buy canvases, Val. I buy masterpieces. I buy the museum.

He turns to the massive farmhouse sink, twisting the brushed-brass faucet. Water flows out in a steady, elegant stream. Arthur scoffs, shoving his hands under the water.

ARTHUR
Look at this flow rate. My koi pond in Malibu has better circulation. You expect me to wash my hands in a trickle?

VALERIE
The property utilizes a state-of-the-art graywater conservation matrix. The eco-efficiency is a major asset for long-term valuation. 

Arthur violently shoves the faucet handle back. The water cuts off. 

ARTHUR
I don't conserve. I consume.

Arthur stalks around the island, closing the distance between them. He invades her personal space, looming over her. 

ARTHUR
This place is supposed to scream 'I own the skyline.' Right now, it's whispering 'I got lucky on a crypto pump.'

His booming voice cracks on the word 'pump', pitching up into a reedy, nervous squeak.

Arthur blinks. He snaps his wrist up, obsessively staring at his heavy Audemars Piguet watch. His eyes dart around the bezel, not registering the time at all.

ARTHUR
Whatever. I'm bored. Let's get out of this glorified cafeteria. I'll buy you a glass of Louis XIII. You look like you could use a taste of the actual good life.

VALERIE
(stepping back, gesturing to the stove)
If we could just pivot to the culinary footprint-- the dual La Cornue ranges offer a staggering return on--

Arthur cuts her off, slamming his hand down on the stovetop. 

ARTHUR
I don't cook! I hire people to cook! And my people need space that doesn't feel like a sterile tomb!

He kicks the baseboard of the island with his designer loafer. 

ARTHUR
The craftsmanship is pathetic. The bones are weak. 

A low, sub-bass HUM vibrates through the floorboards. It travels up through the soles of their shoes. 

Valerie freezes. She looks down at her feet, then to the marble countertop. The surface of the stone seems to subtly vibrate.

VALERIE
The... structural integrity of the foundation is actually over-engineered. It is incredibly robust.

Arthur scoffs. He turns his back to the massive, double oak doors leading out to the hallway. 

ARTHUR
Robust? I could tear this place down to the studs with my bare hands. It's cheap.

He kicks the island again. Harder.

WHAM!

The heavy oak doors behind Arthur violently swing shut. 

They slam into the doorframe with bone-rattling force. The sheer acoustic shockwave knocks a crystal vase off the counter. 

It SHATTERS against the hardwood.

Arthur jumps, his false bravado evaporating instantly. He whirls around to face the closed doors. 

Silence thickens in the kitchen, broken only by the settling dust. 

Arthur reaches out, grabs the brass door handle. He twists it. Pulls. 

It doesn't budge.

ARTHUR
(voice trembling)
Hey. Open up.

He yanks with both hands. The heavy oak remains immovable, like a solid wall. 

Valerie stares at the doors, her perfectly manicured hands trembling at her sides. She vigorously rubs her cuff. 

VALERIE
It’s... a draft. A pressurized draft.

Arthur pounds his fist against the wood. The thud is dull. Dead.

ARTHUR
Open the damn door, Val!

The low hum in the floorboards returns, vibrating up through the soles of their feet. Louder this time. Hungry.

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION DINING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: The pristine hardwood floors groan hungrily beneath their feet, the deep acoustic sound mimicking a massive, starving stomach vibrating through their shoes. Valerie stands completely paralyzed in the center of the room, her eyes darting to the slammed doors, debating whether to drag Arthur out of the terrifying structure and lose the sale forever. She looks at Arthur's terrified, pathetic face, then at the luxurious, multi-million dollar surroundings. Her greed wins out. She physically pivots, blocking Arthur's view of the trapped exit, and launches into an aggressive, rapid-fire sales pitch about the unparalleled acoustic privacy of the dining space, smiling with dead eyes.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: The climax of the sequence where Valerie fully commits to her dark pact, crossing the moral event horizon by ignoring the immediate danger to secure the deal. Micro-Conflict: Valerie's sheer terror of the sentient house versus her desperate need to close the sale with Arthur. Value Shift: From paralyzed terror (-) to aggressive, unnatural salesmanship (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION DINING ROOM - CONTINUOUS

The Brazilian walnut floorboards do not just creak. They GROAN.

A deep, guttural vibration. Low-frequency. The unmistakable, wet acoustic rumble of a massive, starving stomach. It travels straight up through the soles of their shoes.

ARTHUR PENDELTON (30s) freezes mid-step. The obnoxious, booming bravado drains from his face, leaving a pale, sweaty canvas.

VALERIE VANCE (30s) stands dead center under a cascading crystal chandelier. Paralyzed. Her thumb aggressively SCRUBS at the edge of her silk blouse cuff. Back and forth. Erasing a stain that isn't there.

ARTHUR
(voice cracking)
What the hell was that? Is this an earthquake zone? Because my broker swore--

Behind them, the massive mahogany double doors SLAM shut.

The sheer force rattles the crystal above. The heavy iron latch clicks with a wet, metallic finality.

Arthur flinches. He lifts his wrist, staring obsessively at the face of his diamond-encrusted Richard Mille watch. His eyes dart around the dial, not actually reading the time.

ARTHUR
Right. Okay. No. I'm out. The foundation is completely shot. I'm texting my driver to bring the Maybach to the front.

He takes a step toward the slammed doors.

Valerie doesn't move. Her eyes dart from the heavy mahogany exit to Arthur's terrified, pathetic posture. 

She looks at the hand-carved wainscoting. The imported Italian marble fireplace. The sheer, undeniable wealth of the room. She stares at the gold-leaf ceiling, her jaw clenching so hard a muscle twitches in her cheek.

Another GROAN reverberates through the walls. Hungry. Impatient. The dining table shudders.

Valerie stops scrubbing her cuff. Her posture snaps straight. The icy, flawless professional veneer drops back into place, but her eyes are completely dead.

Arthur reaches for the brass doorknob.

Valerie pivots, stepping smoothly and directly into his path. She blocks the doors entirely.

VALERIE
Notice the resonance, Arthur.

Arthur blinks, nearly colliding with her.

ARTHUR
Move, Valerie.

VALERIE
I want you to really listen to the acoustics in this space.

ARTHUR
(pitching higher)
Are you deaf? The house is literally sinking!

VALERIE
It is settling. And as it does, it demonstrates the unparalleled acoustic privacy of the dining sector. 

Arthur tries to sidestep her. Valerie mirrors him, a perfectly synchronized block. She gestures grandly to the walls, her voice sharp and transactional.

VALERIE
Most open-concept layouts suffer from catastrophic noise bleed. But here? The architect integrated custom-baffled subflooring. 

ARTHUR
I don't care about the subflooring! I'm worth four hundred million dollars, I'm not dying in a localized sinkhole!

The floor beneath Arthur's loafers shifts. A subtle, sickening tilt.

He stumbles back, his chest heaving. He taps the face of his watch, frantic, breathless.

ARTHUR
Move away from the door. Now. I'll buy the Balenciaga penthouse. I'll buy the whole damn building, just open the door!

VALERIE
The penthouse is exposed. Common. You are not a common buyer, Arthur. Think about your galas. The exclusivity. You mentioned wanting to host the entire Silicon Valley C-suite. 

She takes a slow, deliberate step toward him. Forcing him backward. Away from the exit. Toward the center of the room.

VALERIE
Imagine the sheer volume of a seventy-person dinner party. The bass from the DJ. Completely contained.

ARTHUR
Stop talking!

VALERIE
(smiling)
You can scream in this room, Arthur. You can scream at the absolute top of your lungs, and not a single soul in the foyer would hear a whisper. That is the premium you are paying for. Total, uncompromised isolation.

Arthur stares at her. The terrifying retail smile plastered on her face doesn't reach her eyes. 

ARTHUR
You're insane. You're actually insane. 

He lunges for the door.

Valerie catches his arm. Her grip is shockingly strong. Her manicured nails dig deep into his cashmere sleeve, anchoring him in place.

VALERIE
We are not done. We are appreciating the investment.

The house responds.

The walls contract. A sickening, wet crunch echoes from the ceiling above them. The chandelier sways violently, raining crystal prisms onto the dining table.

Arthur whimpers. He tries to pull away, but Valerie holds him firmly in the center of the trap. 

VALERIE
Look at the gold-leaf detailing, Arthur. Look at the craftsmanship.

ARTHUR
(hyperventilating)
Let me go!

VALERIE
You wanted a property that commands respect. A property that forces people to notice you. This is it. This house doesn't just make a statement.

The floorboards beneath Arthur's feet begin to soften. The Brazilian walnut warps, turning spongy, sinking under his weight like wet flesh.

Arthur looks down in absolute horror. His loafers are sinking into the wood. The floor is swallowing his ankles.

He looks up at Valerie. Begging.

ARTHUR
Valerie... please.

Valerie slowly releases his arm. She takes a pristine step backward, onto solid wood. Safe.

She casually smooths out her silk cuffs.

VALERIE
It consumes everything else on the market.

Arthur opens his mouth, sucking in a massive breath to scream.

SMASH CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION DINING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Valerie physically pivots, blocking Arthur's view of the trapped oak doors. As the pristine hardwood floors groan beneath them like a starving stomach, she raises her voice, launching into a rapid-fire, aggressive sales pitch about the dining room's 'unparalleled acoustic privacy.' Arthur rolls his eyes, bored and unimpressed, and physically shoves past her to wander out into the hallway.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Establishes Valerie's desperate commitment to the sale over basic safety. (><) Valerie wants to keep Arthur contained and oblivious vs. Arthur's obnoxious restlessness. (+/-) From trapped and panicked (-) to temporarily regaining verbal control (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION DINING ROOM - CONTINUOUS

Valerie stands with her back flush against the grand oak double doors. Her knuckles are stark white around the heavy brass handles. She pulls. Nothing. They are fused shut. 

Beneath her designer heels, the pristine herringbone hardwood vibrates. A deep, guttural GROAN reverberates through the floorboards. It does not sound like settling wood. It sounds exactly like a colossal, starving stomach.

Across the cavernous room, ARTHUR PENDELTON (30s) stops pacing. He wears a cashmere sweater that costs more than most cars. He stares down at his custom Berluti loafers.

ARTHUR
What the hell was that?

Valerie’s right thumb immediately drops to her left cuff. She aggressively scrubs at an invisible stain on the silk. Once, twice. 

She releases the locked handles, forces a painfully flawless smile, and physically pivots, planting herself squarely between Arthur and the trapped doors.

VALERIE
The house breathing, Arthur.

ARTHUR
Breathing? I’m liquidating a massive stock portfolio for this place. If I’m dropping eight figures, the climate control better not sound like a dying Maybach.

VALERIE
(rapid-fire)
It’s the sub-floor radiant heating synchronizing with the smart-home matrix. State-of-the-art thermal expansion. But notice what you don't hear?

Arthur frowns, looking around the empty, cavernous space. 

VALERIE
Echo. 

Arthur lets out a loud, booming laugh that shatters the quiet. 

ARTHUR
I don’t care about echoes, Val. I care about scale. I need acoustics that can handle a live Diplo set without the cops showing up. This feels... dead. Too quiet. Like a crypt.

VALERIE
It’s unparalleled acoustic privacy. 

She steps toward him, commanding the space, deliberately drawing his eye line away from the oak doors. 

VALERIE
Look at the Venetian plaster. The structural density is an architectural marvel. You could host a three-hundred-person gala in the foyer, and in here? Total silence. A sanctuary for your most exclusive negotiations.

ARTHUR
Negotiations. Right. 

He wanders toward the center of the room, running a hand along the custom mahogany dining table. He slaps the wood hard.

ARTHUR
You mean where I tell the board they're fired. 

THUD. 

The entire room seems to flinch from his strike. A low, vibrating HUM begins in the walls. 

ARTHUR
Feels cheap. You sure this isn't a veneer? I'm not paying a premium for a flip.

VALERIE
It is solid, old-growth mahogany. The architectural integrity of this property is absolute. It protects its investments.

ARTHUR
Right. 

He kicks the baseboard with his loafer. 

CRACK. 

The floorboards groan violently in response. A wet, tearing sound echoes from beneath the plaster, like a starved beast snapping its jaws. The massive crystal chandelier above them sways, the glass pendants clinking like chattering teeth.

Arthur freezes. His bravado falters. He aggressively checks his heavy Patek Philippe watch, his eyes glossing right over the dial without registering the time. 

ARTHUR
(voice cracking slightly)
You know what? It’s claustrophobic in here. I’m over the dining room. 

VALERIE
We haven't even discussed the custom millwork. The ROI on a space with this level of--

ARTHUR
(interrupting)
I don't care about the millwork. Where's the infinity pool? The listing said imported Italian travertine. I didn't fly in from Tahoe to look at drywall.

He marches straight toward her. 

Valerie holds her ground, her icy smile frozen in place. She gestures back toward the center of the room, desperate to anchor him.

VALERIE
Arthur, the craftsmanship of this centerpiece alone guarantees a valuation increase that--

ARTHUR
Move.

He doesn't wait for her to step aside. Arthur drops his shoulder and physically shoves past her, his heavy, expensive cologne lingering in the air. 

He ignores the locked oak doors entirely, veering left toward the open, shadowy archway.

ARTHUR
Drinks are on me if the pool doesn't suck! Let's go!

Arthur wanders out into the hallway, his heavy footsteps echoing into the dark. 

Valerie remains in the dining room. She looks down at her trembling hands, then back at the fused oak doors. 

The floorboards beneath her gently ripple. Waiting.

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Arthur struts down the empty corridor, launching into an insufferable tirade about the 'cheap' recessed lighting. He casually leans his full weight against a pristine, load-bearing plaster wall. To mock the construction, he taps the drywall rhythmically, his booming laugh echoing off the high ceilings. Valerie trails behind, gritting her teeth, trying to pivot the conversation back to the architectural value.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Positions the first victim for the kill while highlighting his utter lack of respect for the space. (><) Valerie wants to salvage the pitch vs. Arthur wants to dominate and belittle the environment. (+/-) From annoyed (-) to smugly dominant (+).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS

Arthur’s heavy, custom Berluti loafers slap against the imported Brazilian teak floorboards. He takes up the exact center of the narrow corridor, swinging his arms wide, forcing Valerie to trail half a step behind him in his wake.

ARTHUR
Cheap. It’s just cheap, Val. You feel it? The energy in here is completely low-res. 

VALERIE
(smoothly)
The architect was actually aiming for a minimalist envelope. It’s designed to eliminate visual clutter.

ARTHUR
Minimalist is just a poverty mindset rebranded for rich people. Look at this ceiling.

He points a thick finger upward, not breaking his stride. 

ARTHUR
If I’m dropping eight figures on a property, I expect Viabizzuno tracking systems. Maybe some custom ambient diffusers. This recessed lighting? It looks like a dentist’s office in Pasadena. 

Valerie’s smile remains frozen in place. Her right thumb drops to her left wrist, furiously rubbing at an invisible stain on the cuff of her silk blouse. 

VALERIE
The fixtures are recessed to prioritize the spatial fluidity. It draws the eye directly to the unbroken sightlines of the western exposure. The aesthetic value is entirely in the seamless integration.

Arthur stops dead. Valerie nearly collides with his broad back. 

He pivots, looking at the pristine, hand-troweled plaster wall to his left. He doesn't just touch it. He throws his entire body weight against it, leaning his shoulder into the load-bearing surface and crossing his ankles. 

ARTHUR
Sightlines don't impress my board of directors, Val. Square footage and marble do. You want to talk value? 

VALERIE
I would love to. The ROI on this specific corridor’s footprint—

Arthur interrupts her, raising his fist and rapping his knuckles against the plaster. 

Thump. Thump. 

ARTHUR
You hear that? 

VALERIE
That is authentic Venetian plaster. The structural integrity—

ARTHUR
Integrity? Please. 

He knocks again, harder this time. 

Thump. Thump. Thump. 

ARTHUR
Sounds hollow. Like my ex-wife. 

Arthur throws his head back. A booming, abrasive laugh erupts from his chest. It bounces off the high ceilings, sharp and violent. 

Valerie’s jaw clenches. Her teeth grind together with an audible click. She scrubs at her cuff so hard the delicate silk begins to fray. 

VALERIE
(tightly)
Let’s move toward the primary suite. The natural light there provides a much better narrative for the home’s potential.

Arthur ignores her. He finds a rhythm with his knuckles on the wall. A mocking, syncopated beat. 

Thump-thump. Thump. Thump-thump. 

ARTHUR
I mean, feel this. It’s got give. Drywall shouldn't have give, should it? 

He presses his shoulder blade deeper into the surface. 

ARTHUR
I’m gonna have to gut this entire wing. Rip it straight down to the studs. Put in some real weight. Maybe imported basalt. Something that actually commands respect when you walk past it. 

VALERIE
Gutting the corridor would completely disrupt the architectural narrative of the house. 

ARTHUR
I buy the narrative, I rewrite it. That’s how capital works, Val. 

He taps his wrist, staring at his heavy Patek Philippe watch without actually registering where the hands are pointing. The silence in the hallway stretches, thick and suffocating. 

A faint, almost imperceptible vibration hums through the teak floorboards. It travels up through the soles of Valerie’s stilettos. 

Arthur doesn't notice. He is too busy admiring his own reflection in the darkened window at the end of the hall. 

ARTHUR
Tell you what. Let's get a bottle of Louis XIII. On me. We sit down, we carve twenty percent off the asking price to cover my demolition costs, and maybe I take this oversized coffin off your hands. 

Valerie stares at the wall behind Arthur. The smooth plaster seems to catch the recessed lighting strangely. It looks less like a wall, and more like a lung holding its breath. 

Her bank account is flashing red in her mind. Eviction notices. The smell of cheap apartments. 

She forces her hands to drop to her sides. She smooths her skirt. 

VALERIE
I am confident we can find a mutually beneficial financial compromise. 

Arthur beams, utterly victorious. He smacks the wall one last, punishing time with his open palm. 

ARTHUR
Now we’re talking. 

He pushes off the wall to stand up straight. 

But the wall doesn't let him go. 

The pristine white plaster behind his shoulder blade softens, yielding like wet clay. 

CUT TO:

scenes.scene_heading: INT. MANSION HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.narrative_action: Without warning, the solid plaster beneath Arthur's expensive blazer softens, liquefying into a jagged, fleshy maw. The architectural lines warp violently inward, creating a terrifying vacuum that grips Arthur by the shoulders. He barely manages a wet, confused gasp before the wall violently inhales him. The plaster snaps back into perfect, rigid form, instantly silencing his screams. The hallway falls dead silent.
scenes.dramaturgical_function: Delivers the monster's first on-screen kill and establishes the supernatural threat. (><) Arthur's physical existence vs. the House's sudden, violent hunger. (+/-) From loud and obnoxious (+) to consumed and erased (-).
scenes.draft_text:
INT. MANSION HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS

Arthur struts down the ultra-modern, impossibly long corridor. His heavy footsteps echo off the pristine, bone-white walls. 

Valerie trails two paces behind. Her posture is rigid, her smile practically laminated to her face.

ARTHUR
(booming)
It’s a hallway, Valerie. Not a museum exhibit. I’m looking for a statement piece, and this screams 'high-end dental clinic.' 

Arthur stops, throwing his arms wide. He takes up as much space as physically possible.

ARTHUR
Where do I put the Steinway? I need acoustics that can handle a Chopin Nocturne. If I’m dropping fifty million, I expect the architecture to applaud me when I walk in.

VALERIE
The minimalism is the statement, Arthur. It forces the eye toward the negative space. 

Valerie gestures to the smooth surface beside him, her voice perfectly modulated, masking a deep, frantic desperation to close the sale.

VALERIE
The architect utilized a seamless Venetian plaster. It’s designed to absorb ambient tension. Pure, uninterrupted flow.

Arthur scoffs. He leans his entire, heavy body weight back against the pristine wall. 

ARTHUR
